title: PhпїЅdra
content:
В В В В Hippolytus; PhпїЅdra; Chorus of TrпїЅzenian Women

HIPPOLYTUS
В В В В Lay not thine hand upon me; let me go;
В В В В Take off thine eyes that put the gods to shame;
В В В В What, wilt thou turn my loathing to thy death?

PHпїЅDRA
В В В В Nay, I will never loosen hold nor breathe
В В В В Till thou have slain me; godlike for great brows
В В В В Thou art, and thewed as gods are, with clear hair:
В В В В Draw now thy sword and smite me as thou art god,
В В В В For verily I am smitten of other gods,
В В В В Why not of thee?

CHORUS
В В В В O queen, take heed of words;
В В В В Why wilt thou eat the husk of evil speech?
В В В В Wear wisdom for that veil about thy head
В В В В And goodness for the binding of thy brows.

PHпїЅDRA
В В В В Nay, but this god hath cause enow to smite:
В В В В If he will slay me, baring breast and throat,
В В В В I lean toward the stroke with silent mouth
В В В В And a great heart. Come, take thy sword and slay;
В В В В Let me not starve between desire and death,
В В В В But send me on my way with glad wet lips;
В В В В For in the vein-drawn ashen-coloured palm
В В В В DeathпїЅs hollow hand holds water of sweet draught
В В В В To dip and slake dried mouths at, as a deer
В В В В Specked red from thorns laps deep and loses pain.
В В В В Yea, if mine own blood ran upon my mouth,
В В В В I would drink that. Nay, but be swift with me;
В В В В Set thy sword here between the girdle and breast,
В В В В For I shall grow a poison if I live.
В В В В Are not my cheeks as grass, my body pale,
В В В В And my breath like a dying poisoned manпїЅs?
В В В В O whatsoever of godlike names thou be,
В В В В By thy chief name I charge thee, thou strong god,
В В В В And bid thee slay me. Strike, up to the gold,
В В В В Up to the hand-grip of the hilt; strike here;
В В В В For I am Cretan of my birth; strike now;
В В В В For I am TheseusпїЅ wife; stab up to the rims,
В В В В I am born daughter to Pasiphae.
В В В В See thou spare not for greatness of my blood,
В В В В Nor for the shining letters of my name:
В В В В Make thy sword sure inside thine hand and smite,
В В В В For the bright writing of my name is black,
В В В В And I am sick with hating the sweet sun.

HIPPOLYTUS
В В В В Let not this woman wail and cleave to me,
В В В В That am no part of the godsпїЅ wrath with her;
В В В В Loose ye her hands from me lest she take hurt.

CHORUS
В В В В Lady, this speech and majesty are twain;
В В В В Pure shame is of one counsel with the gods.

HIPPOLYTUS
В В В В Man is as beast when shame stands off from him.

PHпїЅDRA
В В В В Man, what have I to do with shame or thee?
В В В В I am not of one counsel with the gods.
В В В В I am their kin, I have strange blood in me,
В В В В I am not of their likeness nor of thine:
В В В В My veins are mixed, and therefore am I mad,
В В В В Yea therefore chafe and turn on mine own flesh,
В В В В Half of a woman made with half a god.
В В В В But thou wast hewn out of an iron womb
В В В В And fed with molten mother-snow for milk.
В В В В A sword was nurse of thine; Hippolyta,
В В В В That had the spear to father, and the axe
В В В В To bridesman, and wet blood of sword-slain men
В В В В For wedding-water out of a noble well,
В В В В Even she did bear thee, thinking of a sword,
В В В В And thou wast made a man mistakingly.
В В В В Nay, for I love thee, I will have thy hands,
В В В В Nay, for I will not loose thee, thou art sweet,
В В В В Thou art my son, I am thy fatherпїЅs wife,
В В В В I ache toward thee with a bridal blood,
В В В В The pulse is heavy in all my married veins,
В В В В My whole face beats, I will feed full of thee,
В В В В My body is empty of ease, I will be fed,
В В В В I am burnt to the bone with love, thou shalt not go,
В В В В I am heartsick, and mine eyelids prick mine eyes,
В В В В Thou shalt not sleep nor eat nor say a word
В В В В Till thou hast slain me. I am not good to live.

CHORUS
В В В В This is an evil born with all its teeth,
В В В В When love is cast out of the bound of love.

HIPPOLYTUS
В В В В There is no hate that is so hateworthy.

PHпїЅDRA
В В В В I pray thee turn that hate of thine my way,
В В В В I hate not it nor anything of thine.
В В В В Lo, maidens, how he burns about the brow,
В В В В And draws the chafing sword-strap down his hand.
В В В В What wilt thou do? wilt thou be worse than death?
В В В В Be but as sweet as is the bitterest,
В В В В The most dispiteous out of all the gods,
В В В В I am well pleased. Lo, do I crave so much?
В В В В I do but bid thee be unmerciful,
В В В В Even the one thing thou art. Pity me not:
В В В В Thou wert not quick to pity. Think of me
В В В В As of a thing thy hounds are keen upon
В В В В In the wet woods between the windy ways,
В В В В And slay me for a spoil. This body of mine
В В В В Is worth a wild beastпїЅs fell or hide of hair,
В В В В And spotted deeper than a pantherпїЅs grain.
В В В В I were but dead if thou wert pure indeed;
В В В В I pray thee by thy cold green holy crown
В В В В And by the fillet-leaves of Artemis.
В В В В Nay, but thou wilt not. Death is not like thee,
В В В В Albeit men hold him worst of all the gods.
В В В В For of all gods Death only loves not gifts,1
В В В В Nor with burnt-offering nor blood-sacrifice
В В В В Shalt thou do aught to get thee grace of him;
В В В В He will have naught of altar and altar-song,
В В В В And from him only of all the lords in heaven
В В В В Persuasion turns a sweet averted mouth.
В В В В But thou art worse: from thee with baffled breath
В В В В Back on my lips my prayer falls like a blow,
В В В В And beats upon them, dumb. What shall I say?
В В В В There is no word I can compel thee with
В В В В To do me good and slay me. But take heed;
В В В В I say, be wary; look between thy feet,
В В В В Lest a snare take them though the ground be good.

HIPPOLYTUS
В В В В Shame may do most where fear is found most weak;
В В В В That which for shameпїЅs sake yet I have not done,
В В В В Shall it be done for fearпїЅs? Take thine own way;
В В В В Better the foot slip than the whole soul swerve.

PHпїЅDRA
В В В В The man is choice and exquisite of mouth;
В В В В Yet in the end a curse shall curdle it.

CHORUS
В В В В He goes with cloak upgathered to the lip,
В В В В Holding his eye as with some ill in sight.

PHпїЅDRA
В В В В A bitter ill he hath iпїЅ the way thereof,
В В В В And it shall burn the sight out as with fire.

CHORUS
В В В В Speak no such word whereto mischance is kin.

PHпїЅDRA
В В В В Out of my heart and by fateпїЅs leave I speak.

CHORUS
В В В В Set not thy heart to follow after fate.

PHпїЅDRA
В В В В O women, O sweet people of this land,
В В В В O goodly city and pleasant ways thereof,
В В В В And woods with pasturing grass and great well-heads,
В В В В And hills with light and night between your leaves,
В В В В And winds with sound and silence in your lips,
В В В В And earth and water and all immortal things,
В В В В I take you to my witness what I am.
В В В В There is a god about me like as fire,
В В В В Sprung whence, who knoweth, or who hath heart to say?
В В В В A god more strong than whom slain beasts can soothe,
В В В В Or honey, or any spilth of blood-like wine,
В В В В Nor shall one please him with a whitened brow
В В В В Nor wheat nor wool nor aught of plaited leaf.
В В В В For like my mother am I stung and slain,
В В В В And round my cheeks have such red malady
В В В В And on my lips such fire and foam as hers.
В В В В This is that Ate out of Amathus
В В В В That breeds up death and gives it one for love.
В В В В She hath slain mercy, and for dead mercyпїЅs sake
В В В В (Being frighted with this sister that was slain)
В В В В Flees from before her fearful-footed shame,
В В В В And will not bear the bending of her brows
В В В В And long soft arrows flown from under them
В В В В As from bows bent. Desire flows out of her
В В В В As out of lips doth speech: and over her
В В В В Shines fire, and round her and beneath her fire.
В В В В She hath sown pain and plague in all our house,
В В В В Love loathed of love, and mates unmatchable,
В В В В Wild wedlock, and the lusts that bleat or low,
В В В В And marriage-fodder snuffed about of kine.
В В В В Lo how the heifer runs with leaping flank
В В В В Sleek under shaggy and speckled lies of hair,
В В В В And chews a horrible lip, and with harsh tongue
В В В В Laps alien froth and licks a loathlier mouth.
В В В В Alas, a foul first steam of trodden tares,
В В В В And fouler of these late grapes underfoot.
В В В В A bitter way of waves and clean-cut foam
В В В В Over the sad road of sonorous sea
В В В В The high gods gave king Theseus for no love,
В В В В Nay, but for love, yet to no loving end.
В В В В Alas the long thwarts and the fervent oars,
В В В В And blown hard sails that straightened the scant rope!
В В В В There were no strong pools in the hollow sea
В В В В To drag at them and suck down side and beak,
В В В В No wind to catch them in the teeth and hair,
В В В В No shoal, no shallow among the roaring reefs,
В В В В No gulf whereout the straining tides throw spars,
В В В В No surf where white bones twist like whirled white fire.
В В В В But like to death he came with death, and sought
В В В В And slew and spoiled and gat him that he would.
В В В В For death, for marriage, and for child-getting,
В В В В I set my curse against him as a sword;
В В В В Yea, and the severed half thereof I leave
В В В В Pittheus, because he slew not (when that face
В В В В Was tender, and the life still soft in it)
В В В В The small swathed child, but bred him for my fate.
В В В В I would I had been the first that took her death
В В В В Out from between wet hoofs and reddened teeth,
В В В В Splashed horns, fierce fetlocks of the brother bull!
В В В В For now shall I take death a deadlier way,
В В В В Gathering it up between the feet of love
В В В В Or off the knees of murder reaching it.
meaning: About: Algernon Charles Swinburne (April 5, 1837 пїЅ April 10, 1909) was a Victorian era English poet. His poetry was highly controversial in its day, much of it containing recurring themes of sadomasochism, death-wish, lesbianism and irreligion.
